modules.title: Email Basics and Anatomy
modules.content:
## Why Business Email Differs from Personal Messaging

Imagine you receive two messages on the same Tuesday morning. The first is a text from a friend: "hey, running 10 mins late, save me a seat!" The second is a message from a new vendor: "Per our phone conversation, please find the attached service agreement for your review. Let me know if you require any modifications."

Both messages serve a purpose, but they operate under entirely different sets of rules. The first is casual, time-sensitive, and relies on a pre-existing personal relationship. The second is formal, record-keeping-oriented, and establishes a professional tone. 

When you use email for business, you are creating a written record of your professional competence. A business email is not just a quick note; it is a digital letter that can be forwarded, saved, and referenced months or years later. 

The primary difference between personal and professional email communication is **purpose and permanence**. Personal emails are often conversational, immediate, and forgiving of typos. Professional emails, however, must be clear, deliberate, and professional because they often serve as official documentation of decisions, requests, and agreements. 

Before learning how to write a professional email, you need to understand the environment in which you are writing. Just as an architect must understand the materials they build with, a professional communicator must understand the anatomy of the email interface.

## The Anatomy of an Email Interface

When you open your email client—whether that is Microsoft Outlook, Apple Mail, or a web-based platform like Gmail—you are presented with a standard layout. While the colors and button placements may vary, the fundamental components remain the same.

### The Header: Addressing Your Message

The header is the top section of the email where you input who is receiving the message and what it is about. It consists of several distinct fields.

**The Recipient Field (To)**
The **recipient** is the primary person (or people) you are writing to. When you type an email address into the "To" field, you are indicating that this person is the main target of your message and that some action or response is expected from them. 

**The CC Field**
**CC** stands for "Carbon Copy," a term that dates back to the era of typewriters, where a sheet of carbon paper was used to create a duplicate of a document. In modern email, CCing someone means you are sending them a copy of the email for their awareness, but they are not the primary actor. For example, if you are emailing a client about a project timeline, you might CC your manager so they stay in the loop, but you do not expect your manager to reply.

**The BCC Field**
**BCC** stands for "Blind Carbon Copy." When you add an address to the BCC field, those recipients receive the email, but their email addresses are hidden from everyone else. The primary recipients in the "To" and "CC" fields will not know the BCC'd person is reading along. This is highly useful when sending a mass email to a list of clients who should not see each other's email addresses, or when you need to quietly loop in a supervisor without alerting the recipient.

**The Subject Line**
Though we will dedicate an entire chapter to crafting the perfect subject line later, it is important to identify it now as a key part of the header. The **subject line** is a brief summary of the email's contents. It is the first thing the recipient sees and often determines whether they open the email immediately or ignore it.

### The Body: Delivering Your Message

The **body** is the large, blank text area below the header where you type your actual message. In a business context, the body is where structure becomes vital. Unlike a personal text message, a professional email body should generally be organized into three predictable parts:

1. **The Greeting:** Acknowledging the recipient professionally.
2. **The Core Message:** The information you are sharing or the request you are making, broken into readable paragraphs.
3. **The Sign-off:** A professional closing and your contact information.

Later chapters will explore exactly how to write each of these sections, but for now, recognize that the body is where the bulk of your communication happens. 

### The Signature: Your Digital Business Card

At the very bottom of the email is the **signature**. Most email platforms allow you to create an automated signature that attaches to the bottom of every new message you write. 

In personal emails, a signature might just be your first name or a favorite quote. In business emails, a signature acts as a digital business card. A standard professional signature usually includes:
* Your full name
* Your job title
* Your company's name
* Your direct phone number
* A link to your company's website

Having a complete signature saves the recipient from having to look up your contact information if they need to call you or verify who you are.

## A Real-World Scenario: Putting the Pieces Together

To see how these components work in practice, consider a realistic scenario. 

Sarah is an office manager who needs to order new laptops for three remote employees. She needs to communicate with the IT vendor to place the order, inform her boss that the purchase is being made, and securely send the employees' shipping addresses without exposing their personal home addresses to the other employees.

Here is how Sarah uses the email anatomy to achieve her goal:

**Header:**
* **To:** vendor@techsupplies.com
* **CC:** boss@company.com
* **BCC:** employee1@email.com, employee2@email.com, employee3@email.com
* **Subject:** New Laptop Order - Company ABC

**Body:**
> Hi David,
> 
> I would like to order three Model X laptops for our remote team. Please see the shipping addresses below. 
> 
> Thank you for your help with this order.
> 
> Best,

**Signature:**
> Sarah Jenkins
> Office Manager, Company ABC
> (555) 123-4567

In this scenario, Sarah uses the "To" field to address the vendor directly, because he is the one fulfilling the order. She uses "CC" for her boss so he is aware the purchase is happening, but she doesn't expect him to do anything. She uses "BCC" for the three employees so they receive the confirmation email, but their personal email addresses remain hidden from the vendor and her boss. Finally, her signature provides her direct phone number in case the vendor needs to call her about the shipping details.

## Navigating the Email Interface

Beyond the composition window, the email interface is designed to help you manage the flow of information. When you log into your email, you will typically see a navigation pane with several folders. Understanding what these folders do is part of mastering email basics.

### The Inbox

The **Inbox** is the main folder where new, incoming emails arrive. This is your digital mailbox. Emails sit here until you read them, delete them, or move them to another folder. 

### The Sent Folder

Every time you send an email, a copy of it is automatically saved in your **Sent** folder. This is incredibly important in business communication. If a client claims they never received an email from you, you can check your Sent folder to verify that you did indeed send it, when you sent it, and exactly what it said.

### Drafts

If you start writing an email but do not finish it, the email program will automatically save it to the **Drafts** folder. This allows you to step away from a complex message, gather more information, and return to finish it later before sending. Drafting is highly recommended for sensitive business emails, as it allows you to review your tone before a message goes out.

### Reply, Reply All, and Forward

When an email lands in your inbox, you have three primary ways to respond to it or share it:

* **Reply:** This sends a response only to the person who sent you the email. If three people were CC'd on the original email, they will not receive your reply.
* **Reply All:** This sends your response to the original sender *and* everyone who was CC'd on the original email. This feature is useful when you are providing an update that everyone on the thread needs to see. However, it should be used with caution. Accidentally hitting "Reply All" when you only meant to reply to the sender is one of the most common—and embarrassing—mistakes in business communication.
* **Forward:** This takes an email you received and sends it to a brand new recipient who was not on the original message. The new recipient will see the contents of the original email, but will not see the previous recipients' email addresses unless they are included in the body of the text.

## Key Takeaways

*   **Business vs. Personal:** Business emails are permanent records that require a higher level of professionalism, clarity, and structure than personal emails, which are typically casual and conversational.
*   **The Header:** The top section of the email where you define your audience. The "To" field is for primary recipients who need to act, "CC" is for people who need to be aware, and "BCC" is for hidden recipients.
*   **The Body:** The main text area where your greeting, core message, and sign-off live. Structure is vital here to ensure readability.
*   **The Signature:** Your digital business card. It should automatically appear at the bottom of your emails and include your name, title, company, and contact information.
*   **Interface Basics:** Knowing how to use the Inbox, Sent, and Drafts folders, along with understanding the distinct differences between Reply, Reply All, and Forward, is essential for managing daily business communication.

modules.title: Planning Your Message
modules.content:
## The Cost of the Unplanned Email

Imagine you open your **Inbox** on a Monday morning and find an email from a colleague. It is three dense paragraphs long. You read the first paragraph, and it talks about a project that was completed last month. You read the second paragraph, and it mentions a meeting you had last week. By the time you reach the third paragraph, you realize the colleague actually wants you to approve a budget for a completely new project—and they need the approval by noon today. 

Because the colleague buried their request at the very end of a long message, you wasted time reading historical context you didn't need. More importantly, if you had been skimming your emails on a phone between meetings, you might have missed the deadline entirely.

This scenario happens thousands of times a day in workplaces around the world. The problem isn't usually a lack of writing skill; the problem is a lack of planning. 

Because of the **purpose and permanence** of business emails, treating them like casual text messages is a recipe for confusion. Before you type a single word into the **Body:** of an email, you need to know exactly what you want to achieve and who is reading it. Taking just sixty seconds to plan your message can save you and your recipients hours of wasted time and endless reply chains asking for clarification.

In this chapter, we will look at the three foundational steps of planning an email: identifying your primary goal, analyzing your audience, and outlining your core message using a simple but powerful framework.

## Step 1: Identify Your Primary Goal

Every well-written business email has exactly one primary goal. If you try to accomplish too many things in a single email, you will dilute your message and confuse your recipient. 

Before you start drafting, ask yourself: *What is the one thing I want the recipient to do, know, or feel after reading this email?*

Your answer will usually fall into one of four categories:

1. **To request an action:** You want the recipient to do something specific, such as approve a budget, sign a document, or provide data. 
2. **To provide information:** You are sharing an update, a file, or an announcement. You do not necessarily need a response; you simply need the recipient to be aware of the information.
3. **To answer a question:** You are replying to a previous email and providing the specific details the other person asked for.
4. **To build a relationship:** You are checking in, thanking someone, or introducing yourself. The goal is to establish or maintain a positive professional connection.

### The "Reply" Trap

A common mistake beginners make is failing to identify a goal before hitting **Reply**. When you receive an email, it is tempting to immediately start typing a response based on your emotional reaction to the original message. 

Instead, pause. Read the original email, and then ask yourself what your goal is in replying. Are you providing the requested files? Are you asking for a deadline extension? Are you forwarding the email to someone else who can actually answer the question? 

If you cannot state your goal in a single, simple sentence, you are not ready to start writing. For example:
* *Vague goal:* "I need to talk to Sarah about the marketing campaign."
* *Clear goal:* "I need Sarah to approve the final graphics for the marketing campaign by Thursday so we can launch on Monday."

Once you have your single-sentence goal, you have a compass for the rest of your email. Every sentence you write should serve that goal.

## Step 2: Analyze Your Audience

Now that you know *what* you want to achieve, you need to think about *who* you are talking to. In Chapter 1, we looked at the mechanics of the **The Recipient Field (To)**, **The CC Field**, and **The BCC Field**. But deciding which field to use is only half the battle. You also need to adapt your message to the people in those fields.

Analyzing your audience means considering their background, their relationship to you, and how much context they already have. This will directly determine the level of formality you use.

### Factors to Consider

When analyzing your audience, ask yourself these three questions:

* **What is their role and knowledge level?** Does the recipient know the technical jargon of your project, or are they a manager from a different department? If you send highly technical details to a non-technical manager, they will likely get confused and frustrated. Conversely, if you over-explain basic concepts to an expert peer, they may feel you are wasting their time.
* **What is their relationship to you?** Are you emailing a close colleague you talk to every day, or are you emailing a senior executive you have never met? The closer the relationship, the less formality you need. 
* **What is their current workload?** If you know your manager is preparing for a major board meeting, this is probably not the time to send them a long email asking for minor feedback on a non-urgent project. 

### Determining the Level of Formality

Your answers to the questions above will help you determine the appropriate level of formality. In business writing, formality is not about sounding stiff or using big words; it is about showing respect for the reader's time and position. 

Formality exists on a spectrum:

* **High Formality:** Used for senior executives, clients you are pitching to for the first time, or official company-wide announcements. The language is polite, structured, and avoids slang or overly casual phrasing.
* **Medium Formality:** Used for colleagues in other departments, external partners you have worked with a few times, or managers you report to. The language is professional but conversational.
* **Low Formality:** Used for close team members you interact with daily. The language can be brief, and you may use internal shorthand, though you should still maintain basic professional respect.

Let’s look at a concrete scenario to see how audience analysis changes the message.

### Scenario: The IT System Update

Imagine you work in the IT department, and you need to inform the company about a mandatory update to the email system that will happen on Friday night. You need to send two emails about this update: one to the company's executive leadership team, and one to your immediate IT team.

**Audience 1: The Executive Leadership Team**
* *Role:* They do not need to know the technical details of the server update. 
* *Relationship:* You do not interact with them daily. 
* *Formality:* High.
* *What they care about:* Will the system be down? Will it cost money? Will it impact productivity on Monday?
* *Planned approach:* Keep it brief. State that the system will be offline Friday night, explain that it is a security upgrade, and assure them it will be back online by Saturday morning.

**Audience 2: Your Immediate IT Team**
* *Role:* They are the ones executing the update. They need the technical specifics.
* *Relationship:* You work with them every day.
* *Formality:* Low.
* *What they care about:* Who is running the backup? Who is doing the QA testing on Saturday morning? What is the rollback plan if it fails?
* *Planned approach:* Be highly specific. Assign tasks. Skip the broad business justification because they already know why it's happening.

If you swapped these approaches—sending highly technical task lists to the executives, and sending a vague "we are upgrading for security" message to your IT team—both emails would fail. The executives would be confused, and your team would not know what to do. 

By analyzing your audience first, you ensure that you are giving the right people the right amount of information.

## Step 3: Apply the 'Bottom Line Up Front' (BLUF) Framework

Once you have identified your goal and analyzed your audience, you are ready to outline your message. To do this, we will use a framework called **Bottom Line Up Front**, commonly referred to by its acronym, **BLUF**.

In everyday conversation, we often build up to our main point. We provide background, explain our reasoning, and then finally deliver the conclusion or the request at the end. This feels polite and natural in spoken language. 

However, in business emails, burying your main point at the bottom of the message is highly inefficient. Business professionals are busy. They skim their **Inbox** looking for actionable items. If they have to read three paragraphs of background information before figuring out what you want, they will likely get annoyed.

The **BLUF** framework flips this natural tendency on its head. As the name suggests, you put your "bottom line"—your main point, your request, or your key conclusion—at the very beginning of the email. 

### Why BLUF Works

The BLUF framework works because it immediately answers the recipient's most pressing question: *Why are you sending this to me?*

By stating your purpose in the first sentence or two, you allow the reader to instantly understand the context for the rest of the email. If they are busy, they can read the first two sentences, understand what is needed, and act on it immediately. If they need more details, they can continue reading the rest of the email to get the background information.

### How to Outline Using BLUF

When planning your email, structure your thoughts into three distinct sections:

1. **The Bottom Line (The Core Message):** This is your primary goal, stated as clearly and concisely as possible. It usually includes a specific request or a direct piece of information.
2. **The Necessary Context (The Background):** This is the minimum amount of background information the reader needs to understand *why* you are making this request. 
3. **The Next Steps (The Action):** This is what happens next, including any deadlines.

Let’s look at how to apply this using a realistic business scenario.

### Scenario: Requesting a Budget Extension

**The Situation:** 
You are a project manager. You recently realized that the software licenses for your team are going to cost $2,000 more than you originally budgeted for this quarter. You need your department director, Maria, to approve the extra budget by Friday so you can purchase the licenses before they expire.

**Step 1: Identify the Primary Goal**
* *Goal:* I need Maria to approve a $2,000 budget increase for software licenses by Friday.

**Step 2: Analyze the Audience**
* *Role:* Maria is a department director. She controls the budget but does not use the software herself.
* *Relationship:* She is your boss. You need medium-to-high formality.
* *What she cares about:* Why the budget was exceeded, how much money is needed, and when a decision is required. She does not need a list of every software tool.

**Step 3: Outline using BLUF**

Now, we outline the email before writing the final draft. 

* **The Bottom Line:** Request approval for a $2,000 budget increase for Q3 software licenses by this Friday.
* **The Necessary Context:** The vendor increased their enterprise pricing unexpectedly last month. This $2,000 covers the gap for the remaining three months of the year. Without these licenses, the design team cannot access the project files.
* **The Next Steps:** If you approve, I will process the purchase order on Monday morning. Let me know if you need a breakdown of the vendor's new pricing structure.

Notice how this outline is structured. The most critical information—the request and the deadline—is right at the top. The context explains *why* the request is being made, but it does not overshadow the request itself. The next steps give Maria a clear picture of what will happen if she says yes.

When you eventually sit down to write the actual email, you will use this outline to fill in the **The Greeting:**, **The Core Message:**, and **The Sign-off:** sections that we discussed in Chapter 1. Because you planned it using BLUF, the **Body:** of your email will naturally be clear, concise, and respectful of Maria's time.

### When to Use BLUF

The BLUF framework is highly effective for the vast majority of business emails. It is especially critical when:
* Sending an email to senior leadership or busy managers.
* Making a request that requires a deadline.
* Delivering bad news or asking for a favor.
* Writing an email that is likely to be forwarded to others (because the new readers will immediately understand the context).

There are a few rare exceptions where a strict BLUF might feel too abrupt—for example, in a highly sensitive email where you need to carefully build rapport before delivering difficult news. However, as a beginner learning to write professional emails, defaulting to the BLUF framework is the safest and most effective way to ensure your messages are understood.

## Putting It All Together: The Pre-Writing Checklist

Before you move on to drafting your next email, take a moment to run through this simple mental checklist. It summarizes the planning phase we have covered in this chapter:

1. **Goal:** Can I state the primary purpose of this email in one sentence?
2. **Audience:** Who is in the **To:** field, and what level of formality do they require?
3. **BLUF:** What is the single most important piece of information the recipient needs to see first?

If you can answer these three questions, the actual writing process will become infinitely easier. You will no longer stare at a blank screen wondering how to start, because you will have a clear roadmap for your message. 

## Key Takeaways

* **Always plan before you type:** Jumping straight into drafting an email without a plan leads to confusing messages and wasted time for both you and your recipient.
* **Identify one primary goal:** Every business email should have a single, clear goal. If you cannot summarize the goal in one sentence, you are not ready to write.
* **Analyze your audience:** Adapt your message based on the recipient's role, knowledge level, and relationship to you. This determines how much context you provide and the level of formality you use.
* **Use the BLUF framework:** "Bottom Line Up Front" means putting your main point, request, or conclusion at the very beginning of your email, followed by necessary context and next steps.
* **Respect the reader's time:** Planning your message ensures your emails are efficient, actionable, and professional.

modules.title: Subject Lines and Greetings
modules.content:
## The First Five Seconds

Imagine you are scanning your **Inbox** on a Monday morning. You have 47 unread emails, and you only have time to read half of them before your first meeting. How do you decide which ones to open and which ones to leave behind? 

If you are like most professionals, you make a split-second decision based on two things: the **Subject Line** and the sender's name. If the subject line is vague, you skip it. If it looks urgent and specific, you open it. 

In Chapter 1, we explored the anatomy of an email, and in Chapter 2, we looked at planning your message. Now, we focus on the very first words your recipient will read. Because of the **purpose and permanence** of business communication, your email is competing with dozens of others for attention. If your subject line fails, your carefully planned **Core Message** will never be read. 

This chapter will teach you how to craft subject lines that demand to be opened and choose greetings that set the perfect professional tone.

## The Job of a Subject Line

In Chapter 1, we briefly introduced **The Subject Line** as the header that tells the recipient what the email is about. But in a business context, a subject line actually has three distinct jobs:

1. **To grab attention:** It must convince the recipient that your email is worth their time.
2. **To summarize content:** It must accurately reflect what is inside the **Body** of the email.
3. **To be searchable:** It must contain keywords so the recipient can find it later using their email search bar.

To accomplish all three, your subject lines must be concise, descriptive, and searchable. 

### Being Concise

Many email clients, especially on mobile phones, cut off subject lines after about 40 to 50 characters. If the most important part of your subject line falls after that cutoff, the recipient might not see it. 

**Concise** means stripping away unnecessary words. You do not need to write in complete sentences.

* *Too long:* I wanted to ask you about the budget for the upcoming marketing project (71 characters)
* *Concise:* Q3 Marketing Budget Question (29 characters)

### Being Descriptive

A descriptive subject line tells the recipient exactly what to expect. It answers the question, "What is this email about?" Avoid one-word subject lines like "Question" or "Hello," which give no context.

* *Not descriptive:* Question
* *Not descriptive:* Meeting
* *Descriptive:* Question about Tuesday's client meeting agenda
* *Descriptive:* Rescheduling request: Thursday 1-on-1

### Being Searchable

Because of the **permanence** of email, your recipient will likely need to search for your message days, weeks, or months from now. What words will they type into the search bar? 

If you are sending a contract, include the word "Contract." If you are discussing a specific client, include the client's name. 

* *Not searchable:* Here is the document you asked for
* *Searchable:* Signed NDA document for Acme Corp

## Anatomy of a Great Subject Line

One of the most effective ways to write a clear subject line is to use a standard formula: **[Category] + [Specific Detail]**. 

The category acts as a label, telling the recipient the nature of the email. The specific detail tells them exactly what it applies to. 

**Examples:**
* **Action Required:** Approve Q3 travel expenses by Friday
* **Update:** Server maintenance completed
* **Request:** Feedback on draft press release
* **Action:** Sign and return Acme contract
* **FYI:** New parking policy for the main office

Notice how these subject lines use tags like "Action Required" or "FYI" (For Your Information). These tags immediately tell the recipient whether they need to do something or simply read and file the email away.

### A Real-World Scenario

Let’s look at how a subject line changes based on the message. 

**Scenario:** Sarah needs her manager, David, to approve her request to attend a three-day conference next month. 

* *Bad subject line:* Conference
  *(Why it fails: Too vague. David gets hundreds of emails about conferences.)*
* *Bad subject line:* Can I go to the marketing conference in Chicago next month? 
  *(Why it fails: Too long, reads like a full sentence, buries the key detail.)*
* *Good subject line:* Approval Request: Marketing Summit Chicago (Oct 12-14)
  *(Why it works: It uses a clear category tag, states exactly what is being asked, and includes the specific event and dates. David knows instantly what the email is about and can search for "Marketing Summit" later.)*

## Common Subject Line Mistakes to Avoid

Even with the best intentions, certain subject line habits will cause your emails to be ignored, deleted, or flagged as spam. Avoid these common pitfalls:

### 1. The Vague Subject
Subject lines like "Hi," "Checking in," or "Important" tell the recipient nothing. "Important" is especially dangerous because everyone thinks their own email is important. Instead of saying it is important, *show* it by being specific about the urgent matter.

### 2. The Mismatched Subject
If your subject line says "Q3 Budget," but the email body asks for vacation time, you have confused your recipient. This often happens when you hit "Reply" to an old email to save time finding someone's address, but then you change the topic entirely. 

*Fix:* If you are replying to an old email but changing the topic, change the subject line. You can write "New Topic: Vacation Request (was: Q3 Budget)" or simply delete the old subject and write a new one.

### 3. Using ALL CAPS
Writing a subject line in all capital letters—like "URGENT: READ IMMEDIATELY"—is the digital equivalent of shouting. It comes across as aggressive and unprofessional. Additionally, many spam filters are programmed to send all-caps emails straight to the junk folder. Use standard Title Case or Sentence case for your subject lines.

### 4. Overusing "URGENT"
If you mark every email as "URGENT" or "Action Required," your recipients will quickly develop banner blindness. When a truly urgent email arrives, they will treat it like all the others. Reserve urgency tags for situations where a lack of response will cause immediate, measurable problems. 

### 5. Leaving the Subject Line Blank
Never send a business email without a subject line. Blank subject lines are frequently flagged as spam, and more importantly, they signal to the recipient that you did not care enough to summarize your message. It is a quick way to have your email ignored.

## The Power of the Greeting

Once the recipient opens your email, the very first thing they read is the greeting. In Chapter 1, we identified **The Greeting:** as the opening salutation of your email. 

The greeting sets the tone for your entire message. It establishes your relationship with the **recipient** and shows respect for their position. Choosing the wrong greeting can make you look overly casual, inappropriately formal, or even disrespectful.

To choose the right greeting, you need to ask yourself one question: *What is my relationship with this person?*

## Choosing a Greeting Based on Relationship

Business relationships exist on a spectrum from highly formal to casual. Your greeting should match where you are on that spectrum.

### 1. Formal Greetings (External or Senior Recipients)

Use formal greetings when you are emailing someone for the first time, someone outside your company, or someone several levels above you in the corporate hierarchy (like a CEO). 

* **"Dear [Name],"** 
  This is the gold standard for formal business communication. It shows respect and professionalism. 
  * *Example:* Dear Mr. Smith,
  * *Example:* Dear Dr. Jones,
* **"Dear [Title] [Last Name],"**
  If you do not know the person's name, you can use their title or department. However, try to find a name if possible, as people generally prefer to be addressed by name.
  * *Example:* Dear Hiring Manager,
  * *Example:* Dear Customer Service Team,

**A note on titles:** If you are unsure whether a woman prefers "Miss," "Mrs.," or "Ms.," always use "Ms." It is the standard professional title for women in business, regardless of marital status. If you are writing to a man, use "Mr." If the person holds a professional title like Doctor, Professor, or a military rank, use that instead of Mr./Ms.

### 2. Professional but Friendly Greetings (Standard Internal)

This is the most common category. Use these greetings for colleagues, clients you have spoken to before, and managers you interact with regularly. 

* **"Hi [Name],"**
  "Hi" is the workhorse of modern business emails. It is friendly, professional, and universally accepted for everyday communication. It strikes the perfect balance between respectful and approachable.
  * *Example:* Hi Sarah,
* **"Hello [Name],"**
  "Hello" is slightly more formal than "Hi" but still perfectly appropriate for daily use. Use it when you want to be a bit more professional but "Dear" feels too stiff.
  * *Example:* Hello David,

### 3. Casual Greetings (Close Colleagues)

Use casual greetings only with coworkers you know well and speak to daily. If you would comfortably text this person, a casual greeting is fine.

* **"Hi team," / "Hi everyone,"**
  Perfect for emailing a small group of close colleagues.
* **"Morning," / "Afternoon,"**
  A relaxed way to acknowledge the time of day without using a name. Only use this if you have an ongoing, active conversation with someone.
* **No greeting at all**
  In rapid-fire email chains where you are replying multiple times in a few minutes, it is acceptable to drop the greeting entirely and just type your response. However, for any new email, always include a greeting.

## Greetings to Avoid in Business

Just as there are best practices, there are greetings that can damage your professional image. 

### 1. "Hey"
While "Hey" is common in text messaging among friends, it is too casual for most business emails. It can come across as overly familiar or slightly disrespectful, especially to someone you do not know well. Stick to "Hi" or "Hello."

### 2. "To whom it may concern,"
This phrase is outdated and feels cold. It tells the recipient you did not take the time to find out who you were emailing. If you are applying for a job, use "Dear Hiring Manager." If you are emailing a general company inbox, use "Hello [Company Name] Team." 

### 3. Misspelled Names
There is no faster way to start an email on the wrong foot than by spelling the recipient's name wrong. Always double-check the spelling of the recipient's name in the **To:** field against their signature or previous emails. If their name is "Katherine," do not guess and write "Kathy" or "Kate" unless they have specifically signed their own emails that way.

### 4. Overly Familiar Terms
Avoid terms like "Mate," "Buddy," "Darling," or "Sweetheart." These are highly inappropriate in a business setting and can easily be perceived as condescending or unprofessional.

## Matching the Subject Line and Greeting

Your subject line and greeting work together to create the recipient's first impression. They should feel like they belong to the same email. 

If you use a highly formal subject line like "Contract Amendment: Confidentiality Clause," but open with "Hey Dave," the recipient will be confused by the tonal shift. Conversely, if your subject line is "Lunch on Friday?," opening with "Dear Mr. Johnson," will feel unnecessarily stiff and awkward.

**Scenario:** You need to send a project update to your manager, Linda, who you meet with every week.
* *Subject Line:* Update: Website Redesign Phase 2
* *Greeting:* Hi Linda,

This pairing is consistent. The subject line is clear and professional, and the greeting is friendly and appropriate for an ongoing managerial relationship.

## Key Takeaways

* **Subject lines have three jobs:** to grab attention, summarize the content, and be searchable later.
* **Use the formula [Category] + [Specific Detail]** (e.g., "Action Required: Sign Q3 Budget") to write clear, concise subject lines quickly.
* **Avoid common subject line mistakes** like vagueness, ALL CAPS, overusing "URGENT," and leaving the subject line blank.
* **Your greeting sets the tone.** Match your greeting to your relationship with the recipient: formal ("Dear [Name]") for new or senior contacts, friendly ("Hi [Name]") for standard daily communication, and casual only for close colleagues.
* **Never guess a name or title.** Always verify the spelling of the recipient's name and use "Ms." for women unless instructed otherwise. Avoid outdated phrases like "To whom it may concern."
* **Ensure consistency.** Your subject line and greeting should reflect the same level of formality to avoid confusing the recipient.

modules.title: Writing the Body and Managing Tone
modules.content:
## The Anatomy of the Body

Picture an email landing in your **Inbox** with a perfect **Subject Line** and a warm **Greeting**. You open it, anticipating a quick read. But instead of a clear message, you are met with a massive wall of text. The first paragraph rambles about the weather, the second dives deep into company history, and the actual point of the email is buried somewhere near the bottom. 

This is the danger of the **Body**. While the **Header** and **Greeting** get the recipient’s attention, the body is where your message lives or dies. It is the longest part of your email, which means it carries the highest risk of confusing the reader. 

In Chapter 2, you learned how to plan your message by identifying your core purpose. In Chapter 3, you learned how to set the stage with a clear **Subject Line** and an appropriate **Greeting**. Now, it is time to build the actual content. Writing a strong email body requires three things: organizing your outlined thoughts into readable paragraphs, adjusting your tone to sound professional without being robotic, and cutting out filler words and jargon that slow the reader down.

## Translating Outlines into Clear Paragraphs

If you followed the planning steps from Chapter 2, you already have a rough outline of what you need to say. You know your purpose, you know your **recipient**, and you know what you need them to do. The challenge now is translating that outline into natural, flowing text.

Beginner writers often make the mistake of writing the body as one giant block of text. When a reader sees a 300-word paragraph, their brain immediately feels fatigued before they even read the first word. To write clearly, you must break your thoughts into distinct, digestible paragraphs.

A professional email body typically follows a simple three-part structure: the opening, the context, and the details.

### The Opening: State Your Purpose
Your first paragraph should immediately tell the reader why you are writing. Do not waste time with small talk or long-winded introductions. Because email lacks the visual cues of a face-to-face conversation, directness is a sign of respect for the recipient’s time.

If your email is an informational update, say so. If you are asking a question, ask it. If you are following up on a previous meeting, mention that. 

**Example Openings:**
*   "I am writing to request approval for the Q3 marketing budget we discussed yesterday."
*   "This email provides an update on the server migration scheduled for this weekend."
*   "I am following up on the proposal I sent last Tuesday regarding the new software rollout."

### The Context: Provide the "Why"
Once you have stated your purpose, use the next paragraph to give the recipient the background they need to understand your message. This is where you provide the "why." 

However, be careful not to over-explain. Only provide the context that is necessary for this specific recipient to make a decision or understand the situation. If you are emailing a colleague who was in the meeting with you, you do not need to re-explain what the meeting was about. If you are emailing a new client, you may need to provide a sentence or two of background.

### The Details: The Specifics
If your email requires the reader to review data, look at a list of options, or understand specific constraints, put these details in their own paragraph. By separating the details from the context, you make it easier for the reader to scan the email and find the exact information they need.

**Example Structure:**
> **(Opening)** I am writing to request approval for the Q3 marketing budget we discussed yesterday. 
> 
> **(Context)** As noted in our meeting, we need to increase our digital ad spend to keep up with the competitor's recent campaign. The current budget is too small to maintain our market share.
> 
> **(Details)** The proposed budget increase is $5,000. This will be allocated as follows: $3,000 to social media ads, and $2,000 to search engine marketing. 

Notice how this structure moves the reader logically from "why I am writing" to "here is the background" to "here are the exact numbers." 

## Managing Your Tone

Tone is the attitude or emotion conveyed in your writing. In face-to-face conversations, tone is easy to manage—you can smile, adjust the pitch of your voice, or use a relaxed posture. In an email, the recipient cannot see or hear you. They can only read your words. As a result, email tone is notoriously easy to misinterpret.

A short, direct email might be intended as efficient, but the recipient might read it as angry or demanding. A highly formal email might be intended as respectful, but it might read as cold or intimidating. 

To write a professional business email, your goal is to sound polite, confident, and professional without being overly stiff. 

### Sounding Polite
Politeness in email comes from using basic manners and acknowledging the recipient's perspective. Always use "please" when making a request, and "thank you" when someone has provided something for you. 

However, you can be polite without being submissive. Avoid using phrases that make you sound like you are apologizing for existing. 

*   **Less Polite:** "Send me the report."
*   **Overly Submissive:** "I am so sorry to bother you, but if you don't mind, could you maybe send me the report when you have a second?"
*   **Polite and Professional:** "Could you please send me the Q3 report by Friday?"

### Sounding Confident
Confidence in writing comes from using active, direct language. When you use tentative language, you make the reader feel unsure about your message. Beginner writers often use words like "just," "think," "maybe," or "feel" to soften their emails, but this actually undermines their authority.

*   **Lacking Confidence:** "I just think we should maybe change the vendor."
*   **Confident:** "I recommend changing the vendor."

Notice how removing the filler words makes the statement stronger and more professional. You are not demanding that they change the vendor, but you are clearly stating your professional recommendation.

### Avoiding Overly Stiff Language
Many beginners confuse "professional" with "formal." They assume that to sound like a business person, they must sound like a legal document. This leads to overly stiff language that feels unnatural and creates distance between the writer and the reader.

A good business email reads like a professional conversation. Write the way you would speak to a colleague in a meeting. Use contractions (like "I'm," "we've," and "don't") to make your writing sound more human. 

*   **Overly Stiff:** "I am writing to ascertain whether the deliverables have been completed."
*   **Professional and Natural:** "I'm writing to check if the project is finished."

## Eliminating Filler Words and Unnecessary Jargon

Clarity and conciseness are the twin pillars of a great email body. Every word in your email should serve a purpose. When you use filler words or unnecessary jargon, you force the reader to do extra work to understand your message. 

### Cutting Filler Words
Filler words are phrases that add length to your sentences but add no meaning. They often creep into our writing when we are trying to sound "official" or when we are typing exactly as we speak. 

Common email fillers include:
*   "In order to" (Instead, use: "To")
*   "At this point in time" (Instead, use: "Now" or "Currently")
*   "Due to the fact that" (Instead, use: "Because")
*   "I am writing to tell you that" (Instead, just tell them)
*   "Please do not hesitate to contact me" (Instead, use: "Please let me know if you have questions")

Let’s look at how removing filler words improves a sentence.

**Before:** "I am writing to tell you that, in order to complete the project on time, we will need an extension due to the fact that the data was delayed."
**After:** "To complete the project on time, we need an extension because the data was delayed."

The second version is shorter, punchier, and much easier to read. It sounds more confident, too.

### Defining and Eliminating Jargon
**Jargon** refers to specialized words or phrases that are used by a specific profession, industry, or group, but are difficult for outsiders to understand. 

Using jargon is not always bad. If you are emailing a fellow software engineer about a coding issue, using technical jargon is efficient because you share a common vocabulary. However, if you are emailing a client in a different industry, or a colleague in a different department, jargon will confuse them.

A good rule of thumb is to only use jargon if you are 100% certain the recipient will understand it. If you have any doubt, use plain language instead.

**Examples of Jargon vs. Plain Language:**
*   **Jargon:** "We need to leverage our synergies to optimize the deliverables."
*   **Plain Language:** "We need to work together to improve the final product."
*   **Jargon:** "Let's circle back and ideate on that."
*   **Plain Language:** "Let's meet again to discuss that."

When you catch yourself using jargon, ask yourself: "Is there a simpler word that means the exact same thing?" If yes, use the simpler word.

## The Power of Formatting in the Body

Because people tend to scan emails rather than read them word-for-word, how your body looks is almost as important as what it says. Even if you have eliminated filler words and crafted perfect paragraphs, a lack of formatting can still make your email hard to read.

Use the following formatting tools to make your body scannable:

### Bullet Points and Numbered Lists
If you are listing three or more items, use a bulleted or numbered list. Lists pull the eye down the page and make it easy for the reader to absorb multiple pieces of information at once.

**Instead of this:**
> "For the event, we will need to order catering, rent additional chairs, and print the name badges."

**Write this:**
> "For the event, we will need to:
> * Order catering
> * Rent additional chairs
> * Print the name badges"

### Bold Text for Key Information
You can use **bold text** to highlight critical details, such as dates, deadlines, or specific action items. This helps a scanning reader immediately find the most important parts of your email. 

Use bold text sparingly. If you bold half of your email, it loses its impact and just makes the text harder to read.

**Example:**
> "Please review the attached document and provide your feedback by **Friday at 5:00 PM**."

### Short Sentences
Long, complex sentences are the enemy of email clarity. When you write a sentence with multiple commas and clauses, the reader has to work hard to figure out what you mean. Aim to keep your sentences under 20 words whenever possible. If you find a sentence getting too long, look for a place to put a period and start a new sentence.

## A Real-World Scenario

Let’s put all of these principles together. Imagine you are a junior project manager. You need to email a senior manager, Sarah, to tell her that a project is delayed because a vendor was late delivering supplies. You need her to approve a new deadline.

**Here is the poorly written first draft:**

> "Hi Sarah, I am writing to tell you that due to the fact that the vendor was late, we are going to have a delay on the project. At this point in time, we are unable to move forward. I just think that we should maybe push the deadline back. The vendor's deliverables were not optimal and we are leveraging our internal team to fix it. Please let me know your thoughts on this matter ASAP."

**What is wrong with this draft?**
1.  **Filler words:** "I am writing to tell you that," "due to the fact that," "at this point in time."
2.  **Lack of confidence:** "I just think," "maybe push."
3.  **Jargon:** "Deliverables were not optimal," "leveraging our internal team."
4.  **Vagueness:** "A delay," "push the deadline back." By how long? 
5.  **Formatting:** It is one block of text, making it hard to scan.

**Here is the revised, professional version:**

> Hi Sarah,
> 
> I am writing to provide an update on the Project Alpha timeline. 
> 
> Because our vendor delivered the supplies three days late, we cannot meet our original deadline of October 15. We are currently using our internal team to sort and organize the materials that did arrive.
> 
> To ensure we deliver a high-quality product, I recommend extending the deadline to **October 20**. 
> 
> Please let me know if you approve this new timeline by end of day tomorrow.
> 
> Thank you,
> [Your Name]

**Why the revised version works:**
*   **Clear Opening:** It immediately states the purpose of the email (updating the timeline).
*   **Confident Tone:** It states the problem directly and makes a clear recommendation ("I recommend extending...").
*   **No Filler/Jargon:** It uses plain language ("delivered the supplies late" instead of "deliverables were not optimal").
*   **Scannable:** It uses short paragraphs and bolds the proposed new deadline.

## Key Takeaways

*   **Structure your body:** Organize your email into three logical parts: the opening (your purpose), the context (the background), and the details (specifics).
*   **Write for scanning:** Use short paragraphs, bullet points, and bold text to make your email easy to read at a glance.
*   **Balance your tone:** Aim for polite, confident, and natural. Avoid sounding overly submissive or overly stiff. Contractions are perfectly acceptable in business emails.
*   **Cut filler words:** Remove phrases like "in order to," "due to the fact that," and "at this point in time." They add length without adding meaning.
*   **Avoid jargon:** Only use industry-specific terms if you are certain the recipient will understand them. When in doubt, use plain, everyday language.
*   **Keep sentences short:** Aim for sentences under 20 words to maintain clarity and prevent reader fatigue.

modules.title: Calls to Action and Sign-offs
modules.content:
Imagine receiving an email that perfectly explains a new project. The subject line is clear, the greeting is warm, and the body details the entire plan with flawless tone. You read it, nod along, and then... you aren't entirely sure what to do next. Do you need to reply? Are you supposed to review a document? Should you just file the information away? 

An email without a clear next step leaves the recipient hanging. It creates friction, slows down work, and often leads to a follow-up email asking, "What do you need from me?" 

By the time your reader reaches the end of the body, they know *what* you are talking about, but they might not know *what to do*. This is where the final pieces of your email come in. You need to clearly state the next steps and close the message with a professional, recognizable identity. 

## The Call to Action (CTA)

In business writing, a **Call to Action (CTA)** is a specific statement that tells the recipient exactly what you want them to do next. 

Every email you send should have a purpose. As discussed in earlier chapters, planning your message and writing a clear body are how you deliver your information. The CTA is how you fulfill your purpose. If your email’s purpose is to get budget approval, your CTA should ask them to approve the budget. If your purpose is to schedule a meeting, your CTA should ask them to pick a time. 

### Why Your CTA Matters

People receive dozens—if not hundreds—of emails in their inbox every day. They are often skimming. If your desired next step is buried in the middle of a paragraph, it will likely be missed. A clear CTA:
*   **Saves time:** The recipient doesn't have to guess what you want.
*   **Reduces back-and-forth:** It answers the "what now?" question immediately.
*   **Increases response rates:** People are more likely to do something if they are told exactly what to do.

### Characteristics of a Strong CTA

A professional CTA is not a demand, but it is direct. It leaves no room for ambiguity. To write a strong CTA, ensure it has the following characteristics:

1.  **Specific:** State the exact action you want them to take. 
2.  **Measurable:** If they need to review something, tell them how. If they need to reply, tell you what to reply with.
3.  **Time-bound:** Give a specific deadline or timeframe.

### Weak vs. Strong CTAs

Let’s look at the difference between vague, weak CTAs and strong, specific CTAs.

**Weak CTA:** "Let me know what you think."
*   *Why it’s weak:* This is too open-ended. Do you want a casual opinion, or do you want them to check for factual errors? By when do you need their thoughts?
*   **Strong CTA:** "Please reply by Thursday at 3 PM with any edits to the second paragraph."
*   *Why it’s strong:* It specifies the action (reply), the deadline (Thursday at 3 PM), and the exact scope (edits to the second paragraph).

**Weak CTA:** "Click the link to see the document."
*   *Why it’s weak:* It tells them what to do, but not why, or what to do after they click. 
*   **Strong CTA:** "Please review the attached project timeline and confirm if the Friday deadlines work for your team."
*   *Why it’s strong:* It gives a reason to look at the document and a specific action to take once they have looked at it.

### Placement of Your CTA

Where you put your CTA is just as important as how you write it. 

The best place for your CTA is at the very end of the body, usually as its own standalone paragraph. Because people skim emails from top to bottom, placing the action item at the end ensures it is the last thing they read before deciding what to do. It acts as the bridge between your message and their response.

### A Concrete Scenario

Imagine you are emailing a colleague, Sarah, to get her feedback on a presentation you are giving next week. 

*   **Subject Line:** Feedback needed: Q3 Marketing Presentation
*   **Greeting:** Hi Sarah,
*   **Body:** I have finished drafting the slides for the Q3 marketing presentation. I want to make sure the data on slides 4 and 5 aligns with your team's latest numbers before I present to the client on Monday. 
*   **CTA:** Could you please review the attached slides and reply by Friday at noon with any corrections to the data?
*   **Sign-off:** ...

In this scenario, Sarah knows exactly what to do (review slides), what to focus on (data on slides 4 and 5), and when to do it (Friday at noon). 

## Choosing the Right Sign-off

Once you have delivered your core message and stated your CTA, it is time to close the email. The **sign-off** (also known as a valediction) is the short phrase you use right before your name. 

The sign-off might seem like a minor detail, but it plays a major role in managing the tone of your email. As we covered in previous chapters, tone is how your email "sounds" to the reader. Your sign-off acts as the final handshake of your message. If your email is formal but your sign-off is overly casual, it can create a jarring experience for the reader.

To choose the right sign-off, you must first identify the formality of your email. Emails generally fall into three categories: formal, standard/neutral, and casual.

### Formal Sign-offs

Use formal sign-offs when you are emailing someone for the first time, communicating with senior leadership, or addressing a serious or sensitive topic. 

*   **Sincerely,** - This is the gold standard for formal emails. It is safe, traditional, and highly professional. It works perfectly for cover letters, legal correspondence, and first-time introductions.
*   **Respectfully,** - Use this when emailing someone at a much higher level in your company, or when you want to show a high degree of deference. 
*   **Kind regards,** - A slightly warmer alternative to "Sincerely," but still highly professional. It is excellent for formal emails to external clients or vendors.

### Standard/Neutral Sign-offs

Use standard sign-offs for everyday business communication. This applies to most of your emails to colleagues, routine emails to clients, and general internal communications.

*   **Best regards,** - The most versatile sign-off in business. It strikes a perfect balance between professional and friendly. If you are ever unsure what to use, "Best regards," is almost always a safe choice.
*   **Best,** - A slightly shorter version of "Best regards." It is extremely common in day-to-day internal emails. 
*   **Regards,** - A bit more direct and to-the-point. It is professional but can sometimes feel slightly abrupt if the rest of the email isn't warm.

### Casual Sign-offs

Use casual sign-offs only when you have an established, friendly relationship with the recipient. This is for close colleagues or long-term clients with whom you communicate daily.

*   **Thanks,** - Very common in quick, internal emails. However, only use it if you are actually thanking them for something (like their time or a quick reply).
*   **Cheers,** - Highly casual. Best reserved for peers you know well. 
*   **Talk soon,** - Good for scheduling meetings or catching up with a familiar colleague.

### Sign-offs to Avoid

Some sign-offs do not belong in professional business emails. Avoid the following:
*   **Love,** - Too personal for a business environment.
*   **Thx or Rgds,** - Text-message abbreviations look unprofessional. Always spell out the word ("Thanks," or "Regards,").
*   **Yours truly,** - Too outdated and formal for modern business, unless you work in a highly traditional field like law.
*   **Sent from my iPhone,** - This is an automatic default, not a sign-off. While common, it is better to change your mobile signature to include your actual name and a standard sign-off.

## Crafting Your Professional Signature

The final piece of your email is your **signature**. This is the block of text automatically inserted at the very bottom of your emails. 

While your sign-off is the closing word ("Best regards,"), your signature is your digital business card. It provides the recipient with your identity and your contact information. A well-crafted signature makes it easy for the recipient to find you in their inbox, look you up online, or call you directly.

### Essential Elements of a Signature

A beginner-friendly signature does not need to be complicated. In fact, simpler is almost always better. Every professional signature should include the following essential elements:

1.  **Your Full Name:** Use the name you want to be addressed by. If you go by a nickname in the office, it is acceptable to use it, but including your full name helps avoid confusion. (e.g., *Michael "Mike" Ross*).
2.  **Your Job Title:** This tells the recipient what your role is and gives context to your email. (e.g., *Junior Accountant*).
3.  **Your Company Name:** Especially important if you are emailing external clients or vendors. 
4.  **Your Contact Information:** Include at least your primary phone number and your primary email address. Even though you are emailing them, having your email address in the signature makes it easy for them to copy and paste it into their contacts app.

### Optional Elements

Depending on your role, you might also include:
*   **A link to your professional profile:** Such as your LinkedIn URL.
*   **Your company website:** A clickable link to your company's homepage.
*   **Your mailing address:** Required in some countries for legal corporate correspondence, but often omitted for internal emails.

### Formatting Your Signature

Your signature should be easy to read. Use standard font sizes (usually the same size as your email body, or one size smaller) and avoid using too many different colors. 

**Standard Signature Example:**

Best regards, 

Michael Ross
Junior Accountant
Pearson Hardman Financial
mike.ross@pearsonhardman.com
(555) 123-4567

### The Image and Quote Debate

You may see colleagues with large company logos, photos of themselves, or inspirational quotes in their signatures. As a beginner, it is best to avoid these. 

*   **Logos and Photos:** These are embedded as image files. They can make your email file size larger, and they often show up as broken image links or strange attachments in some email clients. 
*   **Quotes:** What is inspiring to you might be distracting or even offensive to someone else. Keep your signature strictly professional and focused on contact information.

## Putting It All Together

The end of your email is not just a place to stop typing; it is the launchpad for the next step. Let’s look at how the CTA, sign-off, and signature work together to create a seamless close to your email.

**Scenario:** You are a new marketing assistant. You need the graphic design team to send you a logo file so you can finish a flyer. 

> **Subject:** Logo file needed for Spring Flyer
> 
> Hi David,
> 
> I am putting the final touches on the Spring Community Flyer, and I need the high-resolution version of the company logo to complete the header. 
> 
> Could you please reply to this email with the PNG file by end of day tomorrow? 
> 
> Best regards,
> 
> **Alex Smith**
> Marketing Assistant
> Bright Future Media
> alex.smith@brightfuture.com
> (555) 987-6543

Notice how the flow works:
1.  The **body** explains the situation.
2.  The **CTA** ("Could you please reply...") gives a specific, time-bound instruction.
3.  The **sign-off** ("Best regards,") sets a standard, professional tone.
4.  The **signature** provides all of Alex's contact details in case David needs to call or look him up.

When these three elements are aligned, the recipient knows exactly what the email is about, what they need to do, and how to reach you if they have questions.

## Key Takeaways

*   A **Call to Action (CTA)** is a specific statement telling the recipient exactly what to do next. Every business email should have one.
*   Strong CTAs are specific, measurable, and time-bound. Avoid vague requests like "let me know what you think."
*   Place your CTA at the very end of the body so it is the last thing the reader sees before deciding to act.
*   Your **sign-off** acts as the final handshake and must match the tone of your email. 
*   Use formal sign-offs (e.g., *Sincerely,*) for new contacts and leadership. Use standard sign-offs (e.g., *Best regards,*) for daily business. Reserve casual sign-offs (e.g., *Thanks,*) for close colleagues.
*   Your **signature** is your digital business card. It should always include your full name, job title, company name, and basic contact information.
*   Keep your signature simple. Avoid unnecessary images, inspirational quotes, and text-message abbreviations.

modules.title: Reviewing, Formatting, and Proofreading
modules.content:
Imagine spending twenty minutes carefully drafting an email to a potential client. You’ve planned your message, written a clear subject line, and crafted a polite greeting. You hit send, sit back, and feel accomplished. 

Ten seconds later, your stomach drops. You look at your Sent folder and realize you wrote "I look forward to hearing form you" instead of "from you." Worse, your email is a massive, unbroken wall of text, and your Call to Action is buried at the very bottom. 

Drafting an email is only half the job. The other half happens during the review phase. Polishing your draft ensures that simple typos don’t undermine your professionalism, that your formatting makes the message easy to read, and that your overall clarity leaves no room for misunderstanding. 

## The Final Self-Review: Checking Your Goal and Emotions

Before you worry about commas and spelling, you need to look at your email as a whole. When we write, we often get caught up in the details of what we are saying. During the review phase, you need to step back and ask two big-picture questions about your draft.

### Does the Email Meet Its Original Goal?

Back in **Planning Your Message**, you defined the purpose of your email. Now that the draft is written, does it actually achieve that goal? 

Read your draft from the perspective of the **recipient**. If your goal was to request a status update on a project, is that request perfectly clear? If your goal was to provide a summary of a meeting, does the email actually contain that summary? 

Look specifically at your **Call to Action**. Is it obvious what the recipient needs to do next? If you read the email and cannot immediately identify the next step, the recipient won’t be able to, either. 

### Is the Email Free of Emotional Language?

In **Writing the Body and Managing Tone**, we discussed how tone can easily be misinterpreted in text. When you review your draft, look for emotional language—words that express strong feelings like anger, frustration, or desperation.

Business emails should be objective and professional. If you are writing while you are upset, your draft might contain passive-aggressive phrases or harsh words. 

**Examples of emotional language to remove:**
*   "I am extremely disappointed that..." (Too emotional. Replace with: "I noticed that...")
*   "You completely ignored my last email." (Accusatory. Replace with: "I am following up on my previous email.")
*   "This is incredibly urgent, I need this right now!" (Desperate/demanding. Replace with: "Could you please provide this by 3:00 PM today?")

If you find yourself writing an email in the heat of the moment, save it as a draft and walk away. Come back to it an hour later and strip out the emotional language before you send it. Your goal is to state facts and request action, not to vent your feelings.

## Formatting Tools to Enhance Readability

Once your email’s goal and tone are in check, it is time to format the **Body:**. Formatting is how you visually organize your text. A well-formatted email guides the reader’s eye to the most important information. A poorly formatted email is exhausting to read.

Think of formatting like organizing a physical desk. If you throw all your papers into one messy pile, it takes a long time to find what you need. If you separate the papers into neat folders and put the most urgent folder on top, the desk is easy to navigate.

### Short Paragraphs

A **paragraph** is a group of sentences focused on a single idea. In business emails, paragraphs should be much shorter than they are in books or academic papers. 

A wall of text is intimidating. When a recipient opens an email and sees a massive block of words, they are likely to skim it or put off reading it entirely. 

**Rule of thumb:** Keep your paragraphs to two to four sentences. If a paragraph gets longer than that, look for a natural place to hit the "Enter" key twice and start a new one. 

**Example of a wall of text:**
> I wanted to reach out regarding the upcoming software transition. As we discussed in the meeting, we will be moving from the old database to the new cloud-based system starting next Monday. This transition will require everyone to log out of their accounts by 5:00 PM on Friday so the IT team can migrate the data over the weekend. You will receive a temporary password on Monday morning to log into the new system. Please let me know if you have any questions about this process or if you need help setting up your new login credentials on Monday.

**Example using short paragraphs:**
> I wanted to reach out regarding the upcoming software transition. 
>
> As we discussed in the meeting, we will be moving from the old database to the new cloud-based system starting next Monday. 
>
> This transition requires everyone to log out of their accounts by 5:00 PM on Friday so the IT team can migrate the data over the weekend. You will receive a temporary password on Monday morning.
>
> Please let me know if you have any questions about this process.

Notice how the second version is much easier to read because the eye gets a break between ideas.

### Bullet Points

When you have a list of three or more items, questions, or steps, use **bullet points**. Bullet points break information out of a paragraph and make it highly scannable. 

**Without bullet points (hard to read):**
> For the project to move forward, we need the final budget approval from you, a list of the team members who will be working on the design phase, and the signed vendor contract.

**With bullet points (easy to read):**
> For the project to move forward, we need the following from you:
> *   Final budget approval
> *   A list of team members for the design phase
> *   The signed vendor contract

Keep your bullet points parallel. This means every bullet point should start with the same part of speech (for example, all starting with verbs or all starting with nouns). 

**Good (Parallel - starting with verbs):**
> **Action items for this week:**
> *   **Review** the attached document
> *   **Send** your feedback to Sarah
> *   **Approve** the budget by Friday

### Bold Text

**Bold text** makes certain words darker and thicker than the surrounding text. In business emails, bold text is a spotlight. Use it to draw the reader’s eye to critical details, such as deadlines, specific requests, or important dates.

However, if you bold too much text, the spotlight loses its effect. Use bold text sparingly—only for the most vital pieces of information.

**Example of effective bold text:**
> Hi David,
> 
> Please review the attached contract. I need your final signature by **Friday, November 12th at 4:00 PM** so we can send it to the vendor over the weekend.
> 
> Thanks,
> Maria

In this example, the recipient can skim the email and instantly know the deadline without reading every word.

## Proofreading Effectively

Proofreading is the final step before sending. It is the process of reading your draft specifically to catch **spelling**, **grammar**, and **punctuation** mistakes. 

*   **Spelling:** Using the wrong letters or order of letters (e.g., typing "teh" instead of "the", or "definitely" instead of "definately").
*   **Grammar:** The rules of how words are put together (e.g., matching singular subjects with singular verbs).
*   **Punctuation:** The marks that clarify meaning, like commas, periods, and apostrophes (e.g., "Let's eat, Grandma" vs. "Let's eat Grandma").

Spellcheck tools are helpful, but they are not perfect. They will not catch words that are spelled correctly but used in the wrong context (called **homophones**). For example, your spellcheck will not flag "I look forward to hearing form you" because "form" is a real word, even though you meant "from."

To catch these tricky errors, you need to proofread actively. Here is a step-by-step process to proofread an email effectively.

### Step 1: Run the Spellcheck, But Don't Trust It Blindly

Most email programs have a built-in spellchecker that will underline misspelled words in red. Run this first to catch obvious typos. However, remember that it will miss context errors (like "form" instead of "from"). 

### Step 2: Read It Out Loud

This is the single most effective proofreading technique. When you read silently, your brain often skips over small words and automatically corrects mistakes in your head. When you read out loud, you force yourself to look at every single word. 

If you stumble over a sentence while reading it out loud, the recipient will likely stumble over it while reading it silently. If you run out of breath reading a sentence, it is too long and needs to be broken into two sentences.

### Step 3: Read It Backwards

If you have a very important email (like a proposal to a new client) and you want to be 100% sure there are no spelling errors, read the email backwards, word by word. 

Start at the last word of your **Signature:** and read each word individually back to the top. Because the sentence structure is broken, your brain cannot fill in missing words or guess what comes next. You are forced to look at the spelling of every single word. 

### Step 4: Check the Recipient Fields and Subject Line

Proofreading isn't just for the **Body:**. Many embarrassing email mistakes happen in the **Header:**. 

*   Check the **The Recipient Field (To)**. Are you sending this to the right person? Is the name spelled correctly?
*   Check **The CC Field** and **The BCC Field**. Is there anyone there who shouldn't be? Remember that **BCC** hides recipients from others, so double-check you aren't accidentally putting a hidden recipient in the CC line.
*   Check **The Subject Line**. Does it accurately reflect the email? Is it free of typos? A typo in the subject line is the first thing a recipient sees.

### Step 5: Verify Links and Attachments

If your email references an attachment, confirm the file is actually attached. If you mention a link, click on it to make sure it goes to the correct webpage. A common email mistake is writing "Please see the attached file" and forgetting to attach the file. 

## Putting It All Together: A Scenario

Let’s look at how the review and formatting process transforms a real email. 

**Scenario:** Sarah needs to ask her manager, John, to approve her travel expenses for an upcoming conference. 

**The First Draft (Before Reviewing and Formatting):**
> Subject: expenses
> 
> Hi John, 
> 
> I am writing to you because I need to get my travel expenses approved for the marketing conference in Chicago next month. I am really frustrated that I have to ask for this so late but the conference dates were just announced and I had to book the flights fast before the prices went up. I need you to approve the budget for the flight which is $450 and the hotel which is $200 a night for three nights and also my meals which will be about $50 a day. I also need to know if I should rent a car or if I should just take an Uber from the airport. Let me know as soon as you can because I dont want to lose the flight price. 
> 
> Thanks,
> Sarah

**The Review Phase:** 
Sarah reads the draft and realizes she included emotional language ("really frustrated"). She also notices her Call to Action is buried, and her list of expenses is hard to read. She decides to format the expenses using bullet points and bold text for the deadline.

**The Final Draft (After Reviewing and Formatting):**
> Subject: Approval needed: Marketing conference travel expenses
> 
> Hi John,
> 
> I am requesting approval for my travel expenses for the marketing conference in Chicago next month. The conference dates were just announced, so I need to book my flights quickly to secure the current pricing.
> 
> Here is the estimated breakdown:
> *   **Flight:** $450
> *   **Hotel:** $200/night x 3 nights = $600
> *   **Meals:** $50/day x 3 days = $150
> 
> I also have a quick question: Should I rent a car at the airport, or should I plan to take a rideshare to the hotel?
> 
> Could you please let me know if this budget is approved by **Thursday at 5:00 PM**? This will ensure I do not lose the current flight price.
> 
> Thanks,
> Sarah

**What changed?**
1.  **The Subject Line** was updated to be specific and clear.
2.  **Emotional language** was removed.
3.  **Short paragraphs** were used to separate the context from the list.
4.  **Bullet points** were used to make the expenses easy to scan.
5.  **Bold text** was used to highlight the deadline.
6.  **Proofreading** caught the missing apostrophe in "dont" (changed to "do not" for a more professional tone) and ensured the Call to Action was clear.

By taking an extra five minutes to review, format, and proofread, Sarah transformed a confusing, emotional draft into a clear, professional business email that John can easily read and approve.

## Key Takeaways

*   Always perform a **final self-review** to ensure your email meets its original goal and is free of emotional, accusatory, or overly urgent language.
*   Use **short paragraphs** (two to four sentences) to avoid intimidating walls of text.
*   Use **bullet points** for lists of three or more items to make your email scannable.
*   Use **bold text** sparingly to highlight critical information like deadlines or specific requests.
*   **Proofreading** is necessary to catch spelling, grammar, and punctuation mistakes that spellcheck will miss.
*   Read your email **out loud** to catch awkward phrasing and missing words.
*   Always double-check the **Header:** (To, CC, BCC, Subject) and verify that any mentioned attachments are actually attached.

modules.title: Common Business Email Scenarios
modules.content:
## The Power of Having a Reliable Starting Point

Imagine this: You hit "Send" on an email, and immediately your stomach drops. You realize you just emailed a confidential client contract to the entire company instead of just your manager. Or, you’ve been waiting three weeks for a vendor to send you a crucial piece of information, your project is stalled, and you need to ask them for an update without sounding desperate or angry. 

These situations trigger a stress response in most professionals. When the stakes are high or the dynamics are awkward, staring at a blank screen can feel paralyzing. How do you introduce yourself to a new executive without sounding inexperienced? How do you follow up without being annoying? How do you say "I messed up" without losing your job?

Throughout the previous modules, you have built a strong foundation. You know how to plan your message, craft a clear **Subject Line**, manage your tone in the **Body**, and proofread your final draft. Now, it is time to apply those skills to the real world. 

In business, certain situations happen over and over again. By studying these recurring scenarios and using standard templates as your starting point, you never have to start from a blank page. You can adapt a proven structure, fill in your specific details, and hit send with confidence. 

## Scenario 1: The Introduction Email

Introducing yourself to a new colleague or client is one of the most common emails you will write. The goal here is to establish a professional relationship, clarify your role, and open the door for future communication. 

### Introducing Yourself to a New Colleague
When you join a new team or company, you will often need to introduce yourself to the people you will be working with. Because internal teams are often busy, this email should be brief, friendly, and focused on how you fit into the workflow.

**What to include:**
*   **Subject Line:** Keep it clear and welcoming. (e.g., *Introduction: [Your Name] - [Your Role]*)
*   **The Greeting:** Use a friendly, professional greeting.
*   **The Core Message:** State your name, your new role, and who you will be reporting to or working closely with. 
*   **The Call to Action (CTA):** Invite them to reach out or mention that you look forward to working with them.
*   **The Sign-off:** Use a warm, standard sign-off.

**Template: New Colleague Introduction**

> **To:** [Colleague Name or Team Email]
> **Subject:** Introduction: Sarah Jenkins - New Marketing Coordinator
>
> Hi Team,
>
